name: Day Night Schedule
description: A world schedule advances through named phases that can drive lighting, spawns, NPC routines, and gameplay gates.
design_notes: A day and night schedule gives the world a public rhythm. The important design choices are which gameplay_clock_id advances the schedule, how phase transitions are announced, which systems subscribe to phase changes, and how save files restore the exact phase without breaking quests, spawns, or weather.
design_purpose: Create predictable pressure and relief cycles through visibility, temperature, enemies, or NPC availability.
Give players planning decisions around travel, crafting, stealth, and resource use.
Provide a shared schedule that other systems can reference instead of each inventing private timers.
player_fantasy: The player feels they are living inside a world that changes even when they are not directly pushing it.

parameters.name: schedule_clock_id
parameters.description: Clock source that owns schedule advancement and resolves pause, acceleration, and server authority rules.

parameters.name: cycle_length_minutes
parameters.description: Real-world minutes required for the schedule to complete one full in-game cycle.

parameters.name: phase_table_id
parameters.description: Data table that defines dawn, day, dusk, night, and any custom phase boundaries.

parameters.name: transition_blend_ms
parameters.description: Blend window used by lighting, audio, weather, and UI subscribers when the phase changes.

parameters.name: accelerated_tick_multiplier
parameters.description: Multiplier used by beds, fast travel, or debug tools when the schedule should advance faster than normal.

edge_cases: A save file loads during a transition blend while lighting has advanced but NPC schedules have not.
Fast travel or sleeping skips over a phase that contains a quest trigger, spawn rule, or survival hazard.
Multiplayer clients join with clock drift and briefly see different enemy or lighting states.
A gameplay gate depends on night while a cinematic or pause state temporarily freezes the local presentation.
common_bugs: Lighting, audio, and weather subscribe to different phase names and become desynchronized after acceleration.
NPC schedules run multiple missed phase events at once after load and teleport actors into invalid spaces.
Server and client clocks round the phase fraction differently, causing doors, vendors, or hazards to disagree.
Save restoration stores only the phase name, losing the precise fraction needed for smooth continuation.
balancing_notes: Long cycles support exploration and planning; short cycles create constant pressure but can make travel feel rushed.
Night hazards should be matched with readable preparation options such as shelter, light, or alternate routes.
If the cycle gates required content, provide a waiting or schedule-skipping affordance so players are not left idle.
accessibility_notes: Do not communicate phase changes only through color temperature; include icons, labels, audio, or environmental cues.
Offer gentler night contrast or visibility settings when the schedule materially affects navigation.

implementation_notes.unity: Represent the schedule as a serializable ScriptableObject phase table plus a runtime schedule_clock_id component. Store cycle_fraction and phase_id in saves, drive lighting through curves, and replicate server phase ticks rather than letting each client integrate independently.
implementation_notes.godot: Use a Resource for phase data and an autoload schedule service that emits phase_changed with previous_phase_id and new_phase_id. Apply visual blends in _process, but advance authoritative schedule ticks through a single clock so pause_state and fast-forward rules stay consistent.
implementation_notes.unreal: Implement the schedule in a WorldSubsystem or replicated Actor with phase_table_id, cycle_fraction, and accelerated_tick_multiplier. Use Gameplay Tags or delegates for phase subscribers, and serialize exact phase progress for save/load rather than only storing day or night.
implementation_notes.web: Keep the world schedule in simulation state and expose read-only phase snapshots to rendering, audio, and UI. For multiplayer or replay, derive phase from server timestamp plus content_version so reconnects and bug reports reproduce the same schedule.

relationships.reason: Day-night schedules provide phase timing that survival systems can use for danger, warmth, and resource pacing.
Temperature mechanics can sample schedule phases to alter exposure and shelter importance.
Rewind systems need explicit rules for restoring schedule phase, event triggers, and already-fired transitions.

example_games.title: Minecraft
example_games.note: Uses a day and night cycle that changes visibility and enemy pressure.

example_games.title: The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild
example_games.note: Uses a world clock for lighting, NPC routines, and scheduled world events.

example_games.title: Stardew Valley
example_games.note: Uses a daily clock to structure farming, shop hours, NPC routines, and deadlines.
